masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through Golf, Vol. 2
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through Golf, Vol. 2
masthead.dek: A fresh round on the links — the majors, the players and their records, and the courses, rules, and quirks of the game. Every answer routes to a player, a course, a country, or a count, reached by reasoning. Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Built fresh against the existing Golf set. Verified to the PGA, LPGA, the USGA/R&A rules, and Britannica.

dimensions: major once played as match play
venue of Europe's last away Ryder Cup win
nationality of golfer who dethroned Woods at No. 1
winner who beat 59-year-old Watson in 2009 Open playoff
country whose women's golf boom traces to a 1998 first major
only sub-60 round in LPGA history
best men's player without a major (runner-up record)
plan.note: Three branches (majors/tournament history, players/records, courses/equipment/rules), each with a reflex-defeater, fresh dimensions distinct from the first set.

questions.q: Three of golf's four men's majors move from course to course each year. Which one is always played at the very same club?
questions.detail: The Masters has been held at Augusta National Golf Club every year since 1934; the U.S. Open, Open Championship, and PGA Championship all rotate venues.
questions.why: People reach for the most tradition-soaked event without sorting which one is fixed in place. The Open is older and the U.S. Open feels most 'American,' but only the Masters has a single permanent home.
questions.more: It was first called the Augusta National Invitation Tournament and took the name 'Masters' in 1939.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Men's major golf championships'; Golf Digest, 'Masters FAQ'

questions.cat: Golf · major held outside the U.S.
questions.q: Three of the men's majors are held in the United States. Which one is regularly staged outside it?
questions.detail: The Open Championship is played on links courses in the United Kingdom; the Masters, U.S. Open, and PGA Championship are all held in the United States.
questions.why: Americans call it the 'British Open,' but the temptation is to name the most famous event rather than the one defined by its location. It is the only major routinely played overseas.
questions.more: Its rota of courses sits in Scotland and England (with Royal Portrush in Northern Ireland added back in 2019).
questions.source: Britannica, 'British Open'; Wikipedia, 'Men's major golf championships'

questions.cat: Golf · major once played as match play
questions.dimension: major once played as match play
questions.q: Today every major is decided by total strokes, but one of them used a head-to-head bracket format until 1957. Which major was it?
questions.detail: The PGA Championship was a match-play event from 1916 through 1957, then switched to 72-hole stroke play in 1958.
questions.why: Match play feels like a Ryder Cup thing, so people forget a major once used it. The Open and U.S. Open were always stroke play; only the PGA changed.
questions.more: Lionel Hebert won the last match-play final in 1957; TV's preference for star-studded final-day leaderboards drove the switch.
questions.source: PGA Championship, 'Match Play and the PGA Championship'; Wikipedia, 'PGA Championship'

questions.q: Only a handful of men have won all four professional majors over a career. Who was the very first to complete that set, finishing it in 1935?
questions.detail: Sarazen completed the career grand slam with his 1935 Masters win, the first man to capture all four modern majors.
questions.why: Hogan, Player, Nicklaus, and Woods all spring to mind first, but each finished the slam later. Sarazen got there first, in the Masters' second year.
questions.more: Only six men have done it: Sarazen, Hogan, Player, Nicklaus, Woods, and Rory McIlroy (2025).
questions.source: Golf Channel, 'Career Grand Slam winners'; PGA.com, 'The Golf Career Grand Slam Explained'

questions.cat: Golf · most recent career grand slam
questions.q: In 2025 a Northern Irishman finally became the sixth man to win all four majors in his career, doing it at Augusta. Name him.
questions.detail: McIlroy completed the career grand slam by winning the 2025 Masters, the missing piece after his U.S. Open, Open, and PGA titles.
questions.why: It is easy to assume the club closed decades ago with Woods. McIlroy spent over ten years chasing the green jacket before joining the list.
questions.more: He became the first European to win the career grand slam.
questions.source: Golf Digest, 'The 5 golfers McIlroy now joins'; PGA TOUR, 'Six names, one elusive club'

questions.q: One golfer holds the record for most second-place finishes in the majors, runner-up 19 times. Which legend came up just short that often?
questions.detail: Nicklaus finished runner-up in the majors 19 times, more than anyone, alongside his record 18 victories.
questions.why: The reflex is to crown the man with the most wins, but this is about losses. The same player who won the most majors also lost the most as runner-up.
questions.more: His 19 seconds spanned 1960 to 1983; combined with his wins, that is 37 top-two major finishes.
questions.source: PGA TOUR, '18 mind-boggling Jack Nicklaus stats'; Golf Compendium, 'Nicklaus 2nd-Place Finishes'

questions.q: What is the largest winning margin in any men's major, a 15-shot blowout at the 2000 U.S. Open?
questions.detail: Tiger Woods won the 2000 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach by 15 strokes, the biggest margin in any men's professional major.
questions.why: People guess single digits because even great major wins are usually close. This blowout shattered a record that had stood since 1862.
questions.more: Woods was the only player to finish under par; Els and Jimenez tied for second at +3.
questions.source: Golf News Net, 'Biggest margin of victory in each major'; Wikipedia, '2000 U.S. Open (golf)'

questions.cat: Golf · venue of Europe's last away Ryder Cup win
questions.dimension: venue of Europe's last away Ryder Cup win
questions.q: Europe last won the Ryder Cup on American soil in a famous 2012 comeback at this Illinois club. Name the course.
questions.detail: Europe overcame a 10-6 final-day deficit to win 14.5-13.5 at Medinah, the 'Miracle at Medinah.'
questions.why: Famous Ryder Cup sites like the Belfry or Brookline come to mind, but the most recent European away win happened at a club many can't place.
questions.more: No team has won an away Ryder Cup since.
questions.source: RyderCup.com, 'Europe's last away win'; Athlon Sports, '2012 Ryder Cup'

questions.q: Until 1979, the United States Ryder Cup team faced a side limited to one region. After 1979 the opponent expanded to include players from what larger area?
questions.detail: The Ryder Cup pitted the U.S. against Great Britain (later Great Britain and Ireland) until 1979, when the team broadened to all of Europe.
questions.why: Fans assume 'Team Europe' was always the format, but for over 50 years it was just Britain. Adding continental Europe revived a lopsided rivalry.
questions.more: Spaniards Seve Ballesteros and Antonio Garrido were the first continental players, in 1979.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Ryder Cup'; Wikipedia, '1979 Ryder Cup'

questions.cat: Golf · first international Masters champion's country
questions.q: The first man not from the U.S. to win the Masters did so in 1961. From which country did that breakthrough champion come?
questions.detail: Gary Player became the first international Masters winner in 1961, edging Arnold Palmer by a stroke.
questions.why: The instinct is to guess a European golf nation, but the first overseas green jacket went to a South African nearly three decades into the event.
questions.more: Player went on to complete the career grand slam by 1965.
questions.source: History.com, 'Gary Player becomes first international Masters champion'; Wikipedia, '1961 Masters'

questions.q: The most Open Championships ever won by one man is six. Which golfer, active around the turn of the 20th century, holds that record?
questions.detail: Vardon won the Open six times (1896, 1898, 1899, 1903, 1911, 1914), more than any other golfer.
questions.why: Modern names like Tiger or Tom Watson come to mind, but the record holder played well over a century ago and is best known today for a golf grip.
questions.more: His six wins spanned three different decades.
questions.source: The Analyst, 'The Open Champions and All-Time Records'; Britannica, 'British Open'

questions.q: The first left-handed golfer ever to win a men's major did it at the 1963 Open Championship. Name this New Zealander.
questions.detail: Bob Charles of New Zealand won the 1963 Open at Royal Lytham, the first lefty to win a major.
questions.why: People picture modern lefties like Mickelson or Bubba Watson, but the trail was blazed 40 years earlier by a player few would name.
questions.more: No other left-hander won a major until Mike Weir at the 2003 Masters.
questions.source: Golf Today, 'Left-handed major championship winners'; Sky Sports, 'left-handed Masters winners'

questions.q: Augusta has seen several left-handed champions. Who was the first lefty to win the Masters, taking the green jacket in 2003?
questions.detail: Canada's Mike Weir won a 2003 playoff to become the first left-handed Masters champion.
questions.why: Mickelson's lefty wins are more famous, but his first came in 2004 — a year after Weir, the Canadian, broke the barrier.
questions.more: Lefties have since won the Masters multiple times via Mickelson and Bubba Watson.
questions.source: Sky Sports, 'left-handed Masters winners'; Golf Today, 'Left-handed major championship winners'

questions.q: The last amateur to win a men's major did it at the 1933 U.S. Open. By what name is that achievement counted — was the last amateur major champion how long ago?
questions.detail: Johnny Goodman won the 1933 U.S. Open as an amateur, the last amateur to win any men's major.
questions.why: Bobby Jones dominates amateur lore, but he stopped competing in 1930. The actual last amateur champion is an Omaha player most fans have never heard of.
questions.more: He won by a single stroke over Ralph Guldahl.
questions.source: USGA, 'Amateur Goodman Wins 1933 U.S. Open'; Wikipedia, 'Johnny Goodman'

questions.q: Tiger Woods held all four majors at once across 2000 and 2001 — but never in a single calendar year. What's the popular nickname for that four-in-a-row feat?
questions.detail: Woods won the 2000 U.S. Open, Open, and PGA, then the 2001 Masters, holding all four at once across two calendar years.
questions.why: People conflate it with the 'Grand Slam,' but that means all four in one year. Tiger's version spanned two seasons, earning its own name.
questions.more: Only Woods has held all four modern majors simultaneously.
questions.source: Golf Monthly, 'What Is The Tiger Slam?'; PGA TOUR, 'Chapter 3: The Tiger Slam'

questions.q: In 1953 one golfer won three of the four majors in a single year but couldn't even attempt the fourth because two events overlapped on the calendar. Which player pulled off that 'Triple Crown'?
questions.detail: Hogan won the 1953 Masters, U.S. Open, and Open Championship; the PGA overlapped with the Open, so he couldn't enter it.
questions.why: It looks like a near-miss grand slam, but the schedule made all four impossible. The reflex name is Tiger or Nicklaus, not the 1950s Texan.
questions.more: Only Hogan and Woods (2000) have won three professional majors in one year.
questions.source: USGA, 'Hogan's Triple Crown Season of 1953'; Wikipedia, 'Triple Crown (golf)'

questions.cat: Golf · host of the first 12 Opens
questions.q: Before St Andrews became the Open's spiritual home, one Scottish club hosted the championship every single year for its first dozen stagings. Which course was that?
questions.detail: Prestwick Golf Club hosted the first 12 Open Championships (1860-1872) and 24 in all before being dropped from the rota.
questions.why: St Andrews has hosted the most Opens overall, but it wasn't the original home. Prestwick ran the show alone at the start.
questions.more: Prestwick last hosted in 1925 and is no longer on the rota.
questions.source: Bunkered, 'Which course has hosted the Open most often?'; Wikipedia, 'List of The Open Championship venues'

questions.cat: Golf · most Open hostings in England
questions.q: Only one country in England has ever hosted the Open at the same English club more than a dozen times; that club has staged it most among English courses. Name the course.
questions.detail: Royal St George's in Kent has hosted the Open 15 times, more than any other English course (most recently 2021).
questions.why: The Open is so Scotland-centric that people forget the English rota. The leading English venue isn't a household name like St Andrews.
questions.more: It first hosted in 1894, the first Open held outside Scotland.
questions.source: Golf Monthly, 'The 14 Open Championship Venues'; The Open, 'Open Venues Guide'

questions.q: The winner of the Open Championship lifts a famous silver trophy whose formal name is the 'Golf Champion Trophy.' By what nickname is it almost always known?
questions.detail: The Open's trophy, officially the Golf Champion Trophy, is universally called the Claret Jug.
questions.why: People know the Masters green jacket but blank on the Open's prize. It is a jug shape, not a cup, made of silver.
questions.more: The original has stayed at St Andrews since 1928; winners get a replica to hold for a year.
questions.source: The Open, 'The Claret Jug: All You Need to Know'; Wikipedia, 'Claret Jug'

questions.q: At his record-setting 1997 Masters, Tiger Woods won by a margin no one has matched at Augusta. By how many strokes did he win?
questions.detail: Woods won the 1997 Masters by 12 strokes over Tom Kite, a tournament record, at age 21.
questions.why: Most Masters are nail-biters decided by a shot or two, so a double-digit margin feels impossible. It also made him the youngest Masters champion.
questions.more: His 72-hole total of 270 was the lowest in Masters history at the time.
questions.source: History.com, 'Tiger Woods wins the Masters'; Fox News, 'April 13, 1997'

questions.cat: Golf · second-most weeks at world No. 1
questions.q: One golfer has spent the most weeks ranked No. 1 in the world by a mile. Who sits a distant second on that all-time list?
questions.detail: Norman has 331 cumulative weeks at world No. 1, far behind Tiger Woods (683) but well ahead of Dustin Johnson and Rory McIlroy.
questions.why: The reflex is to name Tiger, but he's the runaway leader, not the answer. Second place belongs to the Australian, who topped the ranking long before today's stars.
questions.more: Norman held No. 1 for 331 weeks across the late 1980s and 1990s; Dustin Johnson is third around 139 weeks.
questions.source: https://www.golfmonthly.com/tour/which-golfers-have-spent-the-longest-time-as-world-no1

questions.cat: Golf · world No. 1 without a major
questions.q: Only two men ever climbed to world No. 1 without ever winning a major. One was Lee Westwood. Name the other Englishman.
questions.detail: Donald spent 56 weeks at No. 1 starting in 2011 and never won a major; Westwood is the only other player to reach No. 1 major-less.
questions.why: Plenty of stars never won a major, but almost none reached No. 1. Narrowing to that tiny club of two leaves only Donald once Westwood is named.
questions.more: Donald even beat Westwood in a 2011 playoff to take over the No. 1 ranking.
questions.source: https://bleacherreport.com/articles/1240808-luke-donald-lee-westwood-and-the-top-9-golfers-without-a-major-victory

questions.cat: Golf · most career professional tour wins
questions.q: The most career wins by any professional golfer, man or woman, on a major tour belongs to a woman. Who has 88 tour titles?
questions.detail: Whitworth's 88 LPGA wins top everyone, beating the men's PGA Tour record of 82 held by Sam Snead and Tiger Woods.
questions.why: Most people reach for Snead, Woods, or Sorenstam. The all-tour record holder is the LPGA's Whitworth, ahead of the famous men's mark of 82.
questions.more: She won her 88 titles between 1962 and 1985, plus six majors.
questions.source: https://www.lpga.com/news/2025/on-this-date-kathy-whitworth-earn-88th-career-lpga-tour-victory

questions.cat: Golf · career Grand Slam club size (men)
questions.q: Gene Sarazen was the first man to complete golf's career Grand Slam. Counting through 2025, how many men have done it in total?
questions.detail: Sarazen, Hogan, Player, Nicklaus, Woods, and McIlroy are the only six men to win all four modern majors.
questions.why: It feels like a longer list given how many legends there are, but only six men have ever held all four trophies across a career.
questions.more: McIlroy became the sixth member at the 2025 Masters, 11 years after his fourth major.
questions.source: https://www.britannica.com/sports/Who-Has-Won-the-Career-Grand-Slam-in-Golf

questions.q: The first golfer of either sex to be ranked world No. 1 before turning 18 was a woman. Who topped the ranking at 17?
questions.detail: Ko reached No. 1 at 17 years, 9 months in 2015, the youngest of either sex; Tiger Woods was 21 when he first reached men's No. 1.
questions.why: People picture a teenage boy prodigy, but the youngest-ever No. 1 in pro golf is the New Zealander Ko, who beat Tiger's age mark by years.
questions.more: She also became the youngest woman to win a major that same year, at the Evian.
questions.source: https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/sport/golf/31079174

questions.cat: Golf · nationality of golfer who dethroned Woods at No. 1
questions.dimension: nationality of golfer who dethroned Woods at No. 1
questions.q: The player who ended Tiger Woods's record run atop the world ranking in 2004 came from a small Pacific island nation. Which country?
questions.detail: Vijay Singh of Fiji overtook Woods in September 2004, ending a 264-week reign at No. 1.
questions.why: Golf's powers are the U.S., Europe, and South Africa, so a Pacific island answer surprises. Singh's Fiji is the only one that fits.
questions.more: Singh held No. 1 for 32 weeks across 2004 and 2005.
questions.source: https://www.owgr.com/news/2004/september/week-36

questions.cat: Golf · women's Super Career Grand Slam
questions.q: Only one woman has won all five of the LPGA's career major championships. Which Australian completed this 'Super Career Grand Slam'?
questions.detail: Webb is the only player, male or female, to win all five different major titles available during her career.
questions.why: Sorenstam and Park are the reflex guesses, but neither captured all five women's majors. Only Webb did, making her the unique answer.
questions.more: Her seven major wins are the most by any Australian golfer, man or woman.
questions.source: https://www.golfmonthly.com/news/what-is-the-womens-career-grand-slam-and-why-its-slightly-ambiguous

questions.cat: Golf · winner who beat 59-year-old Watson in 2009 Open playo
questions.dimension: winner who beat 59-year-old Watson in 2009 Open playoff
questions.q: When Tom Watson nearly won The Open at 59 in 2009, he lost a playoff. Which player denied him by winning the only major of his career?
questions.detail: Cink beat Watson in a four-hole playoff at Turnberry for his lone major; Watson had needed a par on 18 to win outright.
questions.why: The story everyone remembers is Watson's near-miss, so the actual champion is easy to forget. Cink is the name that's verifiable.
questions.more: Watson, at 59, would have been by far the oldest major champion ever had he held on.
questions.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2009_Open_Championship

questions.cat: Golf · country whose women's golf boom traces to a 1998 firs
questions.dimension: country whose women's golf boom traces to a 1998 first major
questions.q: The first golfer from her country to win a major lit a boom of champions back home. Which nation's women, led by this 1998 pioneer, now flood the LPGA?
questions.detail: Se Ri Pak became the first South Korean to win a major (1998 LPGA Championship), inspiring a generation of Korean stars.
questions.why: Japan or China seem plausible Asian guesses, but the documented women's golf explosion traces specifically to Pak's 1998 breakthrough in South Korea.
questions.more: By later years dozens of South Koreans populated the LPGA's top ranks.
questions.source: https://www.lpga.com/news/2025/on-this-date-se-ri-pak-becomes-first-player-from-republic-of-korea-to-win-a-major-championship

questions.cat: Golf · only sub-60 round in LPGA history
questions.dimension: only sub-60 round in LPGA history
questions.q: Only one player ever shot a 59 in an official LPGA Tour round. Which Swede did it back in 2001?
questions.detail: Sorenstam shot 59 at the 2001 Standard Register Ping, still the only sub-60 round in LPGA history.
questions.why: Several women have shot 60, so a guesser may hesitate, but exactly one broke 60 on the LPGA, and it was Sorenstam.
questions.more: She birdied 12 of her first 13 holes that day.
questions.source: https://www.lpga.com/news/2025/on-this-date-annika-sorenstam-shoots-59-at-2001-standard-register-ping

questions.cat: Golf · best men's player without a major (runner-up record)
questions.dimension: best men's player without a major (runner-up record)
questions.q: Most people name two famous European stars as the best men's player never to win a major. Which Scot, with five major runner-up finishes and eight Order of Merit titles, owns the title in many eyes?
questions.detail: Montgomerie was runner-up in five majors and won the European Order of Merit a record eight times without ever taking a major.
questions.why: Westwood is the other common pick, but the clue's eight Order of Merit titles and five major seconds point squarely at Montgomerie.
questions.more: He won 31 European Tour events across his career.
questions.source: https://sportslens.com/news/5-best-golfers-to-have-never-won-a-major-colin-montgomerie-misses-out-on-top-spot/

questions.cat: Golf · youngest men's major winner ever
questions.q: The youngest man ever to win a major did it in 1868 and still holds the record over 150 years later. Which Scottish prodigy?
questions.detail: Young Tom Morris won the 1868 Open Championship at age 17, a youngest-champion record that still stands.
questions.why: Modern names like Spieth or Woods come to mind, but the all-time youngest is a 19th-century Scot whose record nobody has touched.
questions.more: He then won four straight Opens before turning 22.
questions.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Young_Tom_Morris

questions.q: In 2024 a player tied the LPGA record by winning five tournaments in a row, joining only Nancy Lopez and Annika Sorenstam. Who was it?
questions.detail: Korda won five consecutive LPGA starts in 2024, capped by the Chevron Championship, matching Lopez and Sorenstam.
questions.why: Lopez and Sorenstam are named in the clue as the company, so the answer is the modern player who joined them, Korda.
questions.more: The fifth win was also a major, her second career major.
questions.source: https://www.skysports.com/golf/news/12176/13120701

questions.q: The oldest player ever to be ranked world No. 1 in men's golf held the spot past age 42. Which player set that age mark?
questions.detail: Singh was about 42 years old when last ranked No. 1 in 2005, the oldest player to hold the top spot.
questions.why: Most assume an evergreen like Woods or a recent star, but the oldest man to sit at No. 1 is Singh, who peaked in his 40s.
questions.more: He reached No. 1 in his early 40s after a late-career surge.
questions.source: https://www.golfmonthly.com/features/the-game/how-does-the-official-world-golf-ranking-work-242570

questions.cat: Golf · most career European Tour wins
questions.q: The career win record on Europe's main men's tour belongs to a player with 50 titles. Which charismatic Spaniard tops that list?
questions.detail: Ballesteros won 50 European Tour events, the most all-time on that tour, plus five majors.
questions.why: Faldo, Langer, or Woosnam feel plausible, but the all-time European Tour win leader is the Spaniard Ballesteros at 50.
questions.more: He also won the Order of Merit six times.
questions.source: https://www.europeantour.com/players/seve-ballesteros-8/career-record

questions.q: More women's major championships were won by one player than anyone else in history, with 15. Which American holds that record?
questions.detail: Berg won 15 women's majors, the most ever, ahead of Mickey Wright's 13.
questions.why: Sorenstam and Wright are the reflex answers, but the all-time women's major leader is the earlier star Patty Berg at 15.
questions.more: Her total includes seven Titleholders and seven Western Opens.
questions.source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patty_Berg

questions.q: Tiger Woods's record streak of made cuts on the PGA Tour broke a mark held since the 1940s by which legend?
questions.detail: Woods's 142 straight made cuts broke Byron Nelson's old record of 113, set in the 1940s.
questions.why: The clue is about whose record Woods broke, not Woods himself; the answer is the 1940s legend Nelson, ahead of Nicklaus's 105.
questions.more: Woods's streak ran 1998 to 2005 before ending.
questions.source: https://www.golfchannel.com/news/article-golf-central-blog-remember-when-tigers-pga-tour-cuts-made-streak-ends-142

questions.q: The rules cap how many clubs a player may carry during a round. What is that number?
questions.detail: Rule 4.1b limits a player to a maximum of 14 clubs; carrying more brings a penalty (two strokes per hole in stroke play, capped at four).
questions.why: Many guess a round number like 10 or 12, or assume 'one of each.' The actual cap is the oddly specific 14, in force since 1938.
questions.more: You may start with fewer and add up to 14 mid-round, but you can't borrow a club from another player.
questions.source: R&A Rules of Golf, Rule 4.1b (randa.org/en/players-rule-finder/.../limit-of-14-clubs)

questions.q: Since 2019 the rules give a player only so many minutes to look for a ball before it's declared lost. How many minutes?
questions.detail: The 2019 modernization cut the search window from five minutes to three, to speed up play.
questions.why: The five-minute figure was famous for decades, so reflex says 5. The rule changed in 2019, and the trap is the old number.
questions.more: The clock starts when you begin searching in the area where the ball is thought to be, not when the shot was hit.
questions.source: USGA Rules Modernization, 'Reduced Time for Search Before a Ball Is Lost' (usga.org)

questions.q: On a single hole, scoring three under par has a bird name. What is that term?
questions.detail: Three under on one hole is an albatross, also called a double eagle in the U.S. (e.g., a 2 on a par 5).
questions.why: People reach for 'eagle,' but eagle is only two under. Three under jumps to the much rarer albatross.
questions.more: It is far rarer than a hole-in-one; a hole-in-one on a par 4 also counts as an albatross.
questions.source: Wikipedia 'List of albatrosses in notable tournaments'; All Square Golf glossary

questions.q: Golf's rarest score, four under par on one hole, also has a bird name. What is it?
questions.detail: Four under on a single hole, almost always a hole-in-one on a par 5, is a condor; only a handful have ever been recorded.
questions.why: Few players ever hear this term. After albatross, the natural guess stops, but the bird ladder continues to condor.
questions.more: Most recorded condors came from cutting the corner of a sharp dogleg par 5.
questions.source: All Square Golf glossary 'Condor'; Balls & Bogeys 'Rarest Golf Scores'

questions.q: Augusta National's famous three-hole stretch of 11, 12, and 13 has a reverent nickname. What is it?
questions.detail: Writer Herbert Warren Wind coined 'Amen Corner' in a 1958 Sports Illustrated piece for holes 11, 12, and 13.
questions.why: Many know the Masters but blank on the nickname; some confuse it with a single hole rather than the trio.
questions.more: The par-3 12th, only about 155 yards, is the cruel heart of the stretch.
questions.source: Golf Monthly 'Amen Corner Augusta'; Sports Illustrated (Herbert Warren Wind, 1958)

questions.q: At TPC Sawgrass, the nerve-shredding short par 3 surrounded by water is the course's signature. Which hole number is it?
questions.detail: The 137-yard par-3 17th at the Stadium Course is golf's most famous island green, the centerpiece of The Players.
questions.why: It feels like a finishing hole, so people guess 18; the island green is actually the penultimate 17th.
questions.more: It has the highest scoring average of any sub-150-yard par 3 in the ShotLink era.
questions.source: CBS Sports 'TPC Sawgrass 17th hole'; PGA Tour hole stats

questions.q: Oakmont's most photographed hazard is a giant bunker ribbed with grassy ridges, sitting between holes 3 and 4. What is it called?
questions.detail: The Church Pews bunker spans over 26,000 square feet with about a dozen turf ridges resembling pews, flanking the 3rd and 4th fairways.
questions.why: Players know Oakmont is brutal but may not recall the bunker's name; the 'pew' ridges are the giveaway-free clue.
questions.more: It was formed by connecting six separate bunkers between the 1927 and 1935 U.S. Opens.
questions.source: Golf.com 'Oakmont church pew bunker explained'; USGA video

questions.q: When a ball is hit out of bounds, the rules send the player back to replay from the prior spot. How many penalty strokes does this 'stroke and distance' add?
questions.detail: Under Rule 18.2, an out-of-bounds ball is played under stroke and distance: one penalty stroke plus replaying from the previous spot.
questions.why: Because you also lose all the distance, many assume it's a two-stroke penalty. The added penalty is only one; the second 'cost' is the lost distance, not a stroke.
questions.more: So a tee shot OB means your next shot off the tee is playing your third.
questions.source: R&A Rules of Golf, Rule 18.2; USGA Rule 18 interpretations

questions.q: The rules set a maximum head size for a driver, measured by volume. What is that cap in cubic centimeters?
questions.detail: Since 2004 the USGA/R&A have capped clubhead volume at 460 cc (plus a small 10 cc tolerance).
questions.why: Sounds like it should be a round 500; the actual ceiling is the specific 460 cc most modern drivers crowd right up against.
questions.more: A separate rule caps the face's spring-like effect (COR) at 0.830.
questions.source: USGA Equipment Rules; 'History of Equipment Rules' (usga.org)

questions.q: A modern Titleist Pro V1 ball is covered in small dimples that control its flight. Their count sits closer to which figure: about 400, about 800, or about 1,200?
questions.detail: The current Pro V1 carries 388 dimples in a tetrahedral pattern; counts across brands generally run roughly 300 to 400-plus.
questions.why: People often picture far more dimples than there are. The real number is in the few hundreds, not the thousands.
questions.more: Titleist tested over 60 dimple patterns before settling on this one.
questions.source: Golf Digest 'Titleist Pro V1 2021'; Titleist Newsroom

questions.q: The rules set a MINIMUM diameter for a golf ball, not a maximum. What is that smallest legal diameter in inches?
questions.detail: A conforming ball must be at least 1.680 inches (42.67 mm) across; there is no maximum size.
questions.why: The twist is direction: the rule is a floor, not a ceiling, so a ball can be bigger but never smaller than 1.68 inches.
questions.more: A smaller 1.62-inch 'British ball' was legal in the R&A game until 1990.
questions.source: USGA Equipment Rules; Sizes.com 'golf'

questions.q: Pebble Beach's most photographed hole is a tiny par 3 perched above the Pacific, the shortest hole on the PGA Tour at about 106 yards. Which hole number is it?
questions.detail: The downhill par-3 7th, roughly 106 yards, is the shortest hole on Tour and one of the most photographed in golf.
questions.why: Short par 3s feel like back-nine drama, so people guess a higher number; this jewel is the 7th, on the front nine.
questions.more: At the 2010 U.S. Open it was shortened to about 92 yards for the final round.
questions.source: Golf Monthly 'Pebble Beach famous par-3 facts'; theScore

questions.q: The U.S. state with the most golf courses is Florida. Which state ranks second, helped by year-round play out west?
questions.detail: Florida leads with about 1,260 courses; California is second with roughly 1,000, per the National Golf Foundation.
questions.why: Texas, with its size, is a tempting guess, but California's golf density edges it for the runner-up spot.
questions.more: Florida has roughly 31 percent more courses than California.
questions.source: National Golf Foundation State-by-State Report (sgbonline.com)

questions.q: Royal Calcutta, founded in 1829, is the oldest golf club outside Britain. In which country does it sit?
questions.detail: Royal Calcutta Golf Club in India, founded in 1829, is the oldest golf club outside the United Kingdom.
questions.why: People expect the United States or a Commonwealth outpost like Australia; the answer is colonial-era India.
questions.more: Golf reached India through British officers decades before it spread to America's clubs.
questions.source: Wikipedia 'Royal Calcutta Golf Club'; Golf Club Atlas

questions.q: On the Old Course at St Andrews, the deep, sprawling bunker that swallows balls on the long 14th hole has an ominous name. What is it called?
questions.detail: Hell Bunker, roughly 25 yards wide and about 10 feet deep, guards the par-5 14th on the Old Course.
questions.why: St Andrews is famous for the Road Hole bunker at 17, so that's the reflex; the named monster on the 14th is Hell Bunker.
questions.more: Jack Nicklaus once took four shots to escape it during an Open.
questions.source: Golf Monthly 'Bunkers of St Andrews'; Golf Digest 'St Andrews landmarks'

questions.q: At the 1999 Open, Jean van de Velde melted down in a creek fronting the 72nd green that locals call the Barry Burn. Which Scottish course was it?
questions.detail: Van de Velde's triple bogey at Carnoustie's 18th, where the Barry Burn winds across the hole, cost him the 1999 Open.
questions.why: St Andrews is the reflex Scottish links, but this notorious finish happened at Carnoustie.
questions.more: He lost the resulting playoff to Paul Lawrie, who'd started the day ten shots back.
questions.source: ESPN 'How Jean van de Velde threw away the Open'; Top100GolfCourses 'Carnoustie'

questions.q: Most full 18-hole courses have four par-3 holes and four par-5 holes. How many par-4 holes does that leave on a standard par-72 layout?
questions.detail: A typical par-72 course has 4 par-3s, 4 par-5s, and the remaining 10 holes as par-4s (4x3 + 10x4 + 4x5 = 72).
questions.why: It's a quick arithmetic trap: 18 minus 8 leaves 10, but people blurt 8 or 9 before doing the subtraction.
questions.more: Par-4s are the backbone of nearly every regulation course.
questions.source: USGA course rating standards; standard scorecard layout

questions.q: The maximum penalty for carrying too many clubs in stroke play is capped no matter how many extra holes you play. What is that maximum stroke total?
questions.detail: Carrying more than 14 clubs costs two strokes per hole in stroke play, but the total is capped at four strokes for the round.
questions.why: People assume it stacks per hole all round; the rule caps the damage at four strokes total.
questions.more: In match play it's a deduction of holes, capped at two.
questions.source: R&A Rules of Golf, Rule 4.1b penalty provisions

questions.q: A golfer who shoots even par for 18 holes on a par-72 course posts what total score?
questions.detail: Even par on a par-72 course is a score of 72, since each hole's par sums to 72.
questions.why: The phrase 'even par' makes some hunt for a fancier number; even par simply equals the course par, 72.
questions.more: Shooting your handicap or better usually means beating this baseline by several strokes for an amateur.
questions.source: USGA scoring fundamentals; standard course par definition

questions.q: The rules limit how deep a club face's grooves may be cut, to curb spin from the rough. That depth ceiling is about which fraction of an inch: 0.02, 0.2, or 2?
questions.detail: A groove's depth may not exceed 0.020 inch (0.508 mm); width is capped at 0.035 inch.
questions.why: The grooves are far shallower than people picture; 0.2 inch would be a canyon, so the tiny 0.02 figure wins.
questions.more: A 2008 groove rule change reduced spin from the rough for elite players.
questions.source: USGA Equipment Rules, Rule 6 Club Face; groove measurement procedure (usga.org)
